risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "with half of the children randomly assigned to a concurrent 14-day course of oral ciprofloxacin (Bayer Healthcare AG) and half to oral placebo. Children were randomized to one of two maintenance treatment strategies" Comment: The paper clearly states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g. computer-generated list, random number table, block randomization, stratification, central randomization). 'Randomized' alone without further detail or context about sequence generation is insufficient to confirm a truly random method.

Quote: "with half of the children randomly assigned to a concurrent 14-day course of oral ciprofloxacin (Bayer Healthcare AG) and half to oral placebo. Children were randomized to one of two maintenance treatment strategies" Comment: The paper confirms random assignment, but it does not describe any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g. central randomization, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). Because the method used to prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment is not reported, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "all children received initial Pa eradication treatment consisting of 28–56 days of open label tobramycin inhalation solution (TIS..." and "Children were randomized to one of two maintenance treatment strategies... (1) Cycled therapy: treatment provided in quarterly cycles regardless of findings from scheduled quarterly respiratory cultures, or (2) Culture-based therapy: treatment only in response to identification of Pa from quarterly cultures." Comment: The trial explicitly states that TIS was open-label, and the maintenance strategies (cycled vs culture-based treatment) would be known to participants and treating personnel because treatment frequency depended on culture results. At least some important outcomes, especially pulmonary exacerbation and treatment with antibiotics, are susceptible to influence by knowledge of treatment assignment and clinical management decisions, so lack of blinding could plausibly affect outcomes.

Quote: "all children received initial Pa eradication treatment consisting of 28–56 days of open label tobramycin inhalation solution (TIS)" and "Assessment for a pulmonary exacerbation was done at all scheduled study visits as well as acute illness visits. An a priori, established definition of pulmonary exacerbation was used for the determination of these events." Comment: The most vulnerable outcome was pulmonary exacerbation, which was assessed at study/illness visits by clinicians/investigators (clinician judgement using a predefined definition), not by an automated system. Because treatment included open-label TIS and the trial compared cycled versus culture-based strategies, outcome assessors were likely aware of allocation. Pulmonary exacerbation assessment/treatment decisions are judgement-dependent and could be influenced by this knowledge. Although P. aeruginosa culture outcomes were processed centrally and are objective, the study also included this unblinded clinician-assessed subjective outcome, so by the worst-outcome rule the domain is High risk.

Quote: "A total of 282/304 (93%) participants in the EPIC clinical trial were included in the study cohort, based on the availability of the initial eradication outcome at week 10. The 22 excluded children were similar to those included in the current analysis except for a slightly younger age and lower BMI..." Comment: This reports 22 post-randomization exclusions before the week-10 eradication outcome was available (about 7% overall), but the paper does not provide the per-randomized-arm numbers analysed for the outcomes of interest during the 15-month maintenance phase, nor reasons for these missing week-10 outcomes by arm. The subsequent exacerbation analyses are reported on 282 participants, but no CONSORT-style per-arm follow-up/analysis counts at the analysed time points are given. Because the article does not let us assess whether missing outcome data were balanced across randomized groups or whether later attrition occurred on the analysed outcomes, attrition bias is unclear rather than high.
